Basically, they own all the contractual conversations.

We do have solution engineers that basically demo the platform, walk customers through what our offerings are, and get a better understanding of their needs and their use cases.
very for and of I'll
We try to, from there, we try to come up with a joint value statement in terms of, okay, these are the type of like capabilities we can offer, but ultimately, what is it?
What are the gaps in your business today that you're hoping to close out using our tools?
So our sales team usually does a good job coming up with those specific use cases, then we go into the POC phase of using their use cases, everything that they have flagged as a gap today.
Okay, what solutions we can use to show them kind of like the value.
So we usually focus on the most protocol, like low hanging, but also like impactful use cases.
We run a POC with the team, usually we're being compared, like we're running against two or three other competitors in the space.
So that's like how our customers try to like say, okay, are you a better fit or someone else?
So we run our POC, we also try to like get an understanding of what the competitors are doing.
And at the end of it, the customer.

So we'll provide a readout in terms of, okay, like, this is what we did for you during the POC, these were the goals that we established at the beginning, here is basically what we're reporting out in terms of the stats that we've been tracking.

And then the customer basically does a readout afterwards, so they meet with every vendor who's participating in the POC individually, at the end of the day, they come back to us with a readout of what they liked or didn't like.
And then we either close the deal, or the deals fall through.
Once the POC phase is done, it goes into implementation phase, we do have implementation managers, tech leads.
They start integration, integration is a big part of what we do, so we integrate with all the different tools that we need to integrate with on the customer side, different vendors.
And basically, they set up all the solutions that the customer has signed up for.
And during the implementation phase, it's to basically stand up the automation and show some initial value, right?
If it's some automated workflow, we want to be able to prove some value.
In that phase, towards the end of the implementation phase, they bring in customer success managers, which is my team.
So we start working closely with the implementation manager to understand that joint value statement, plus everything that has been implemented.
And then we come in and we talk to customers about, okay, moving forward, what do we want to focus on?
Obviously, we want to keep doing well on all the areas that we established from the POC and going into the implementation, anything new that was surfaced.
But then long-term strategy, where are we heading?
Like this is what we're working on today, but where are we heading later?
That's where we come in, just to make sure basically we're taking over from implementation.
Once the technical pieces are done, and then we're ongoing enablement, training, identifying any other areas that we can potentially, you know, bring in more values.

We also have account managers that we work closely with to basically tell them, hey, we also noticed like this area or that area could really be beneficial to this customer if you want to come in and basically pitch your upsell at that point.

And then CSMs also work closely with product teams, because in the existing solution, if there are any gaps, we try to work with product to work on some product enhancements, closing out the gaps in existing offerings.
And then in a lot of cases, there are also other use cases that we may not be supporting today in the product.
